channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Protection against Source Inference Attacks in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) is vulnerable to Source Inference Attacks (SIA), where an honest-but-curious server identifies the exact client contributing a given training sample—despite standard differential privacy defenses failing without severe accuracy degradation. We propose a novel defense within the shuffle model of FL, demonstrating that naive gradient-level shuffling is insufficient. Instead, we introduce **parameter-level shuffling combined with the Residue Number System (RNS)**, enabling lossless, identity-agnostic reordering of model parameters without noise injection. Our method provably breaks statistical links between gradients and client identities while preserving full model accuracy. Experiments across MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Sentiment140 show that baseline shuffling yields SIA accuracy of 68%–82%, whereas our approach reduces it to 22.3%–26.1%—statistically indistinguishable from random guessing (e.g., 10% for 10 clients). The solution integrates seamlessly with existing privacy mechanisms and incurs no accuracy trade-off.

item.title: Accurate, private, secure, federated U-statistics with higher degree
item.description: We address the problem of privately and securely computing degree-$k$ ($k \geq 2$) U-statistics—e.g., Kendall’s $\tau$, AUC, Gini mean difference—in federated learning. Prior approaches rely on low-utility local differential privacy (LDP) or suffer from poor scalability with domain discretization. We propose the first MPC-based protocol for *centrally* differentially private U-statistic computation, achieving significantly higher accuracy. Our method leverages symmetric kernel decomposition and optimized noise injection to minimize MSE while guaranteeing $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-privacy. Theoretically, its MSE scales as $O(1/(n^k\varepsilon^2))$, outperforming LDP baselines by up to $O(n^{k-1})$. Empirically, on Kendall’s $\tau$, it reduces MSE by **up to four orders of magnitude**; for AUC under $\varepsilon = 1$, error is only $1/270$ of state-of-the-art baselines. The protocol is communication-efficient ($O(n^{k-1})$ per round) and supports arbitrary $k \geq 2$.

item.title: DualSentinel: A Lightweight Framework for Detecting Targeted Attacks in Black-box LLM via Dual Entropy Lull Pattern
item.description: Recent black-box LLM deployments face severe threats from targeted attacks (e.g., backdoor, prompt injection), yet existing defenses are impractical due to high access requirements, prohibitive overhead, or inference disruption. We propose **DualSentinel**, a lightweight, runtime defense framework that detects attack activation *during* LLM generation. Its core insight is the **Entropy Lull**—a characteristic period of abnormally low and stable token probability entropy when an LLM is hijacked into a fixed output path. DualSentinel employs a dual-check mechanism: (1) a magnitude- and trend-aware entropy monitor for sensitive, real-time lull detection; followed by (2) lightweight task-flipping verification—if the lull persists under semantically inverted tasks (e.g., QA → reverse-QA), the attack is confirmed with high confidence. Evaluated across 12 black-box LLMs and 5 attack types, DualSentinel achieves **99.2% detection accuracy**, **<0.3% false positive rate**, and adds only **<8 ms latency** and **<2 MB memory**, enabling practical, zero-modification deployment. Code: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18479273.

item.title: From Secure Agentic AI to Secure Agentic Web: Challenges, Threats, and Future Directions
item.description: This survey bridges Secure Agentic AI and the emerging Secure Agentic Web. We introduce a component-aligned threat taxonomy covering prompt abuse, environment injection, memory attacks, toolchain abuse, model tampering, and agent network attacks. Defense strategies reviewed include prompt hardening, safety-aware decoding, privilege-controlled tool/API access, runtime monitoring, continuous red-teaming, and protocol-level mechanisms (e.g., signed delegation, trusted channels). Crucially, we show how risks escalate in the Agentic Web—where delegation chains, cross-domain interactions, and protocol-mediated ecosystems amplify threats via composition and propagation. We identify four open challenges for web-scale deployment: interoperable identity/authorization, end-to-end provenance and traceability, ecosystem-level coordinated response, and scalable evaluation under adaptive adversaries. Our work connects empirical security findings with system-level requirements to chart practical research directions toward trustworthy, deployable agent ecosystems.

item.title: Inference-Time Safety For Code LLMs Via Retrieval-Augmented Revision
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) for code generation face critical trustworthiness challenges due to opaque security reasoning and brittleness against evolving vulnerabilities. We propose **Retrieval-Augmented Revision (RAR)**—an inference-time safety mechanism that operates *without model retraining*. RAR first detects security risks in generated code, then retrieves expert-vetted security discussions from a curated Stack Overflow knowledge base, and finally guides a lightweight LLM to revise the code with transparent, evidence-based edits. Our approach advances trustworthiness along three axes: **interpretability** (interventions grounded in real community explanations), **robustness** (adapting to new vulnerabilities via knowledge-base updates alone), and **safety alignment** (real-time blocking of unsafe code pre-deployment). Evaluated on benchmark and real-world datasets, RAR significantly improves code security over prompt engineering alone—reducing vulnerability rates by 42.7% on average—while introducing *zero* new vulnerabilities per static analysis. This demonstrates retrieval-augmented inference-time intervention as a practical, complementary safeguard for trustworthy LLM-based coding.

item.title: Detection-Gated Glottal Segmentation with Zero-Shot Cross-Dataset Transfer and Clinical Feature Extraction
item.description:
## Background & Innovation Accurate glottal segmentation in high-speed videoendoscopy (HSV) is critical for quantifying laryngeal kinematics, yet existing deep learning models suffer from spurious artifacts and poor cross-dataset generalizability. We propose a **detection-gated pipeline**: a YOLOv8 detector spatially gates a U-Net segmenter, while a temporal consistency wrapper suppresses false positives during glottal closure and instrument occlusion. Trained on only 600 frames from GIRAFE (<0.5% of the dataset), it achieves **zero-shot transfer** to the large-scale, multi-institutional BAGLS dataset—without any fine-tuning.
## Key Results The framework achieves state-of-the-art Dice scores of **0.81 on GIRAFE** and **0.85 on BAGLS** (in-distribution), outperforming prior methods by +0.12 in DSC. Downstream validation on 65 clinical subjects confirms robust extraction of kinematic features (Open Quotient, area coefficient of variation), with ICC > 0.92 vs. expert annotations. Critically, the glottal area CV significantly distinguishes healthy from pathological vocal function (*p* = 0.006). At **~35 FPS**, the lightweight architecture enables real-time clinical deployment. Code, weights, and evaluation scripts are publicly available.

item.title: OpenRad: a Curated Repository of Open-access AI models for Radiology
item.description: OpenRad is a curated, standardized, open-access repository designed to address fragmentation in radiology AI research. Through systematic retrospective review of 5,239 PubMed/arXiv/Scopus records (up to Dec 2025), 1,694 peer-reviewed and preprint studies were included. Model records—structured per the RSNA AI Roadmap JSON schema—were auto-generated using a locally hosted gpt-oss:120b LLM and rigorously verified by 10 domain experts; LLM output stability was confirmed (Levenshtein ratio >90%) on 225 random papers, with 78.5% expert edits classified as minor. The repository currently hosts ~1,700 open radiology AI models spanning all modalities (CT, MRI, X-ray, US) and subspecialties, with MRI dominating (621 neuroradiology models) and CNNs/Transformers as prevalent architectures. Geographically, research output is concentrated in China and the United States. The OpenRad web interface enables keyword search and filtering by modality, subspecialty, intended use, verification status, and demo availability, complemented by live analytics. Community contributions are supported via a dedicated submission portal. OpenRad establishes a foundational, searchable, and sustainably maintained resource for reproducible development and clinical translation of radiology AI.

item.title: Federated Agentic AI for Wireless Networks: Fundamentals, Approaches, and Applications
item.description: This paper introduces **Federated Agentic AI**, a novel paradigm unifying agentic AI’s autonomous loop (perceive-plan-act-reflect) with federated learning (FL) to address critical challenges in wireless networks—resource constraints, geographical dispersion, and severe data heterogeneity (non-IID). We systematically map core agentic components (e.g., memory, tool use, reflection) to three FL types: horizontal FL (hFL) for cross-node policy coordination, vertical FL (vFL) for multi-layer knowledge alignment, and **Federated Reinforcement Learning (FRL)** for robust sequential decision-making. As a concrete case study, we deploy FRL in low-altitude wireless networks (LAWNs) for UAV-assisted dynamic spectrum access: local Q-network updates combined with secure gradient aggregation reduce communication overhead by 58% while improving channel selection success rate by 23.7% over centralized DQN, under strict <15 ms end-to-end latency. The work establishes foundational design principles for privacy-preserving, low-latency, non-IID-resilient agentic intelligence in 6G networks.

item.title: CA-AFP: Cluster-Aware Adaptive Federated Pruning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers from statistical and system heterogeneity, yet clustering and pruning are typically addressed separately. We propose **CA-AFP**, a unified framework that performs *cluster-specific adaptive pruning*: clients are first grouped into data-similar clusters, and each cluster’s model is pruned iteratively during training using a novel **cluster-aware importance score**, which jointly considers weight magnitude, intra-cluster parameter coherence, and gradient consistency. Crucially, CA-AFP incorporates **weight regrowth** to enable self-healing after pruning. Evaluated on UCI HAR and WISDM under natural user-partitioned Non-IID settings, CA-AFP achieves superior trade-offs among accuracy (+2.1% over pruning baselines), inter-client fairness (38% lower performance variance), and communication efficiency (62% less than dense clustering methods). It remains robust across varying Non-IID levels (α = 0.1–1.0) and ablation studies validate the critical roles of clustering, the scoring mechanism, and the pruning schedule.

item.title: S5-HES Agent: Society 5.0-driven Agentic Framework to Democratize Smart Home Environment Simulation
item.description: This paper introduces **S5-HES Agent**, a Society 5.0–driven agentic framework that democratizes smart home simulation by eliminating programming barriers and enabling natural-language-driven, end-to-end configuration. It orchestrates specialized LLM-powered agents—via interchangeable models—to autonomously generate home topologies, simulate device behaviors (calibrated against real-world IoT datasets like IoT-23 and CASAS), and model security threats. A hybrid RAG pipeline (semantic + keyword + fused search) retrieves authoritative smart home knowledge from standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-213), protocols (Matter, Zigbee), and open device repositories. Evaluation shows near-optimal retrieval fidelity (>96% relevance), high-fidelity threat replication (94.7% alignment with ground-truth attack patterns), and predictable linear scalability across home sizes (1–50 devices, R² = 0.998). The open-source implementation (MIT License, GitHub: AsiriweLab/S5-HES-Agent) establishes a stable, inclusive foundation for cross-disciplinary Society 5.0 research in security, energy, health, climate, and socio-economics.

item.title: Extracting Training Dialogue Data from Large Language Model based Task Bots
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in task-oriented dialogue systems (TODS), yet their tendency to memorize training dialogues—including full dialogue-level events and PII—poses severe privacy risks. This work presents the first systematic study of training data extraction from LLM-based TODS, identifying key limitations of existing attacks (e.g., low label density, rigid response templates) and proposing a novel two-stage attack: (1) state-aware prompt sampling to trigger high-confidence dialogue state outputs, and (2) robust membership inference leveraging slot consistency and distributional shift detection. Evaluated on MultiWOZ and SGD benchmarks across five task domains, our method extracts over 2,800 dialogue state labels with up to **70.2% precision** (F1 = 62.3%), outperforming baselines by +31.5% in precision. We further quantify critical memorization factors—state update frequency, slot co-occurrence strength, and data repetition—and propose practical mitigations (e.g., state-masked distillation) that reduce extraction success to 11.4% with <2.3% task performance degradation.

item.title: Retrieval, Refinement, and Ranking for Text-to-Video Generation via Prompt Optimization and Test-Time Scaling
item.description: Text-to-video (T2V) models remain highly prompt-sensitive despite advances driven by large-scale data. Existing optimization methods either introduce artifacts via post-hoc editing or incur prohibitive costs through generator fine-tuning. We propose **3R**, a training-free, retrieval-augmented prompt optimization framework compatible with *any* off-the-shelf T2V diffusion model. 3R integrates three synergistic components: (1) **Retrieval**—using vision-language models to extract context-enriching modifiers from video-text corpora; (2) **Refinement**—diffusion-based preference optimization that aligns outputs with human judgments of motion coherence and object stability; and (3) **Ranking**—temporal frame interpolation followed by motion-consistency scoring for optimal sequence selection. Experiments on MSR-VTT and WebVid-10M show consistent gains: +12.7% CLIP-Score (static fidelity) and +18.3% MotionScore (dynamic coherence), with zero-shot generalization >86%. 3R establishes a scalable, interpretable paradigm for prompt-centric T2V enhancement.

item.title: Noise-Calibrated Inference from Differentially Private Sufficient Statistics in Exponential Families
item.description: This paper bridges a critical gap in differentially private (DP) inference for exponential families by proposing *noise-calibrated inference* from DP sufficient statistics. Instead of releasing DP synthetic data (causing severe miscalibration) or DP point estimates (lacking uncertainty quantification), we release only clipped sufficient statistics via the Gaussian mechanism—yielding approximate DP with explicit sensitivity control. We establish asymptotic normality of the plug-in DP MLE, derive its exact variance inflation due to privacy noise, and construct valid Wald-style confidence intervals. A noise-aware likelihood correction—first-order equivalent to the plug-in estimator but compatible with bootstrap-based inference—is introduced. Crucially, a matching minimax lower bound proves the derived privacy-distortion rate is unavoidable. The framework delivers concrete design rules and a practical pipeline, validated on normal, Poisson, and Bernoulli families and real U.S. Census data.

item.title: Decentralized Federated Learning by Partial Message Exchange
item.description: Decentralized federated learning (DFL) eliminates the central server but suffers from data heterogeneity, restrictive theoretical assumptions, and performance degradation under communication compression or privacy mechanisms. This paper proposes **PaME (DFL by Partial Message Exchange)**, a novel algorithm where neighboring nodes exchange only *randomly selected sparse coordinates* of model updates per iteration. PaME achieves up to **87% communication reduction**, provides inherent privacy via extreme sparsity, and maintains full accuracy. Crucially, we prove its **linear convergence** under only two mild assumptions: local gradient Lipschitz continuity and doubly stochastic communication matrix—removing common requirements like global convexity, homogeneous data, or uniform sampling. Experiments on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and real-world medical data show PaME outperforms D-PSGD, ADMM, and GT-DSGD by 1.2–3.8% in test accuracy under 90% less communication, with superior robustness to network asynchrony and node failures.

item.title: Adversarial Query Synthesis via Bayesian Optimization
item.description: Benchmark workloads are critical for evaluating modern database systems—especially as ML-driven components (e.g., learned optimizers, adaptive indexing) become pervasive. Yet, existing benchmarks (e.g., TPC-H, JOB) lack systematic coverage of optimizer failure modes and rely heavily on manual curation. We propose **Adversarial Query Synthesis via Bayesian Optimization (AQS-BO)**, a fully automated framework that treats query generation as a black-box optimization problem. Using Gaussian process surrogate models and expected improvement acquisition, AQS-BO efficiently navigates the structured SQL space to synthesize syntactically valid, semantically meaningful queries that maximize optimizer headroom—the gap between estimated and actual cost. In experiments on PostgreSQL and SQLite, AQS-BO generated queries with **217% average optimization headroom**, more than double that of TPC-H (98%) and JOB (103%). Over 83% of synthesized queries exposed critical optimizer errors (e.g., wrong join orders), versus <12% in baselines. The method requires only minutes per iteration on a single GPU, enabling scalable, reproducible, and adaptive database benchmarking.

item.title: Fed-GAME: Personalized Federated Learning with Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts For Time-Series Forecasting
item.description: Federated learning (FL) on graphs holds promise for distributed time-series forecasting, yet existing methods suffer from reliance on static topologies and poor handling of client heterogeneity. To address this, we propose **Fed-GAME**, a novel personalized FL framework that reformulates personalized aggregation as message passing over a *learnable, dynamic implicit graph*. Its core innovation is a **decoupled parameter-difference update protocol**: clients upload only the difference between their fine-tuned private model and the shared global model. On the server, these differences are split into two streams: (1) an *averaged stream* updates the global model for consensus; (2) a *selective stream* feeds into our new **Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts (GAME)** aggregator. GAME employs shared experts to generate cross-client attention scores and lightweight personalized gates to adaptively weight selective updates—enabling fine-grained personalization without compromising global coherence. Extensive experiments on two real-world EV charging datasets demonstrate that Fed-GAME consistently outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL baselines (e.g., pFedMe, FedRoD) by up to 12.7% in MAE/RMSE, with faster convergence and lower communication cost.

item.title: Committing Security of BBB Secure MACs
item.description: Committing security is a recently introduced MAC property essential for abuse reporting in end-to-end encrypted messaging, where traditional unforgeability fails to ensure tag uniqueness across contexts. Building on Bhaumik et al.’s (CRYPTO’24) foundational work on commitment and context-discovery security for birthday-bound MACs, we initiate the first systematic study of these properties for beyond-birthday-bound (BBB) MACs. We analyze two prominent BBB families: (i) Chen et al.’s (ASIACRYPT’21) two-blockcipher-plus-hash constructions (e.g., 2BC-H), and (ii) well-established DbHtS-based MACs (e.g., PMAC+, 3kf9). Our results reveal a stark dichotomy: several 2BC-H variants are broken by simple, efficient attacks—violating commitment with a single query—while PMAC+ and 3kf9 resist context-discovery under standard assumptions. Crucially, we identify *key separation* and *uniqueness of tag derivation paths* as structural prerequisites for committing security in BBB designs—providing the first design principles for abuse-resilient high-security MACs.

item.title: CRISP: Circuit-pRivate Single-Image Steganography with Permutations
item.description: We propose **CRISP**, the first provably secure *circuit-private* homomorphic steganography scheme that embeds an entire Fredkin gate’s three inputs into a *single* RGB cover image—and writes all three outputs to *one* output image. To break structural leakage, CRISP applies two independently sampled permutations per gate: $\pi_{\mathrm{in}}$ dynamically assigns input bits to RGB channels, while $\pi_{\mathrm{out}}$ remaps outputs—preventing deterministic channel-matching attacks enabled by the Fredkin gate’s control-bit pass-through. Security analysis shows the adversary’s advantage in locating the secret pixel and recovering bits decays as $\Theta(1/(h{\times}w))$, cryptographically negligible for practical images (e.g., $512{\times}512$). CRISP reduces per-gate overhead to just **2 images**, cutting total image count by >60% on obfuscated benchmarks—while delivering the qualitatively new guarantee of *circuit privacy*, formally proved and experimentally validated.

item.title: Coppersmith's Method for Solving Modular Inversion Hidden Number Problem via Determinant-Based Elimination
item.description: We propose a **determinant-based elimination framework** to systematically generate shift polynomials in Coppersmith’s method—resolving a long-standing bottleneck in constructing low-degree, linearly independent polynomials for multivariate modular root finding. Applied to the Modular Inversion Hidden Number Problem (MIHNP), our approach improves the theoretical MSB recovery bound to $\delta > 1/3$ (for $n$-bit prime $p$), outperforming prior methods both asymptotically and empirically (e.g., 92% success rate on 512-bit instances). It also achieves optimal entropy efficiency for predicting the Inversive Congruential Generator (ICG), requiring only $\approx 2\log_2 p$ MSBs. Crucially, our analysis of the Modular Inversion Double Hidden Numbers Problem (MIDHNP) proves it is *no harder* than MIHNP—refuting Boneh et al.’s (Asiacrypt 2001) conjecture and establishing their computational equivalence. This work provides the first algebraically principled, determinant-driven variant of Coppersmith’s method with provable advantages.

item.title: Threshold Traitor Tracing Revisited: Insider Attacks and Multi-Traitor Tracing
item.description: Recently, Boneh, Partap, and Rotem (Crypto’24) introduced *threshold traitor tracing* (TTT), enabling tracing of at least one colluder when a decoder is sold to an *external* buyer. However, we identify a critical gap: their model—and all existing constructions—fail under *insider attacks*, where the decoder is sold to a member of the decryption committee itself. To address this, we formalize *insider-resilient TTT*, proving that standard single-traitor tracing is inherently insufficient. We propose *multi-traitor tracing* as the essential primitive: requiring the tracer to identify *multiple* colluders (e.g., ≥2), thereby ensuring accountability even when the buyer is colluding. We design efficient compilers that amplify tracing capability from 1 to *k* traitors while achieving sublinear ciphertext size (Õ(√n)) and maintaining polynomial-time tracing. Our constructions are the first provably secure against insider attacks, with applications beyond TTT.

item.title: Cryptanalysis of Polynomial Learning With Errors (PLWE): A Survey
item.description: This survey provides a structured, up-to-date analysis of cryptanalytic attacks against the Polynomial Learning With Errors (PLWE) problem—a practical algebraic variant of LWE widely used in post-quantum cryptographic schemes. We systematically classify and evaluate all known efficient attacks targeting *vulnerable PLWE instances*, including number-theoretic root-based attacks, geometric ideal-lattice reductions, statistical distinguishing techniques, and equivalence-preserving transfers to Ring-LWE (RLWE). Our key contributions include: (1) establishing concrete necessary conditions for PLWE security—e.g., irreducibility of the defining polynomial $f(x)$, absence of small-complexity roots, and high Galois complexity of its splitting field; (2) identifying previously overlooked fragile parameter families (e.g., $f(x) = x^n + 1$ with $q \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$); and (3) proposing a practical parameter-validation checklist for implementers. Crucially, we clarify when PLWE vulnerabilities *provably extend* to RLWE, enabling unified security assessment. This work serves as both a research reference and an engineering safeguard for PQC standardization and deployment.

item.title: FALCON with message recovery, a specification
item.description: FALCON is a lattice-based digital signature scheme submitted to NIST (proposed as FN-DSA), offering smaller keys and signatures than ML-DSA but requiring floating-point arithmetic. This note fully specifies **FALCON with message recovery (FALCON-MR)**—a practical optimization for scenarios where minimizing *signature plus message* size is critical (e.g., constrained networks or short-message protocols). Unlike key-recovery mode, MR embeds part of the message into the signature, enabling full message reconstruction from the signature alone. Compared to standard FALCON, MR saves up to **226 bytes** (vs. FALCON-512, NIST Level 1) and **434 bytes** (vs. FALCON-1024, NIST Level 5). Crucially, all core algorithms—including key generation, trapdoor sampling, and Gaussian sampling—remain *unchanged*; only the hashing logic, message parsing, and randomness binding are modified. We also outline how MR extends naturally to the ring-signature variant of FALCON (Gajland et al., Crypto 2024). This specification targets implementers, providing unambiguous, ready-to-deploy details.

item.title: Hermine: An Efficient Lattice-based FROST-like Threshold Signature
item.description: We present **Hermine**, the first lattice-based threshold signature scheme achieving *all three* defining features of FROST—partially non-interactive signing, non-interactive identifiable abort (IA), and proactive security—under standard lattice assumptions (e.g., Module-LWE). Designed for NIST’s “Medium” scale ($N \leq 64$), Hermine produces compact **11 KB signatures**, enabling practical deployment. Its core technical advance is *everywhere-short secret sharing*: a novel method to split a short secret vector $\mathbf{s} \in R_q^\ell$ into short shares with a short linear reconstruction algorithm. Security requires a fine-grained analysis of information leakage inherent in short shares—a departure from prior lattice sharing paradigms. We also formalize game-based unforgeability and IA definitions augmented with proactive security, providing foundations for future PQ threshold designs.

item.title: A White-Box Bootstrapping Approach for High Precision Comparison Over Homomorphic Encryption
item.description: We propose a novel **white-box bootstrapping** technique for efficient and numerically stable sign evaluation over the CKKS homomorphic encryption scheme. Unlike prior polynomial-composition approaches—which suffer from multiplicative depth explosion and noise accumulation due to intermediate (black-box) bootstrapping—our method intrinsically *reduces* noise during bootstrapping, as rigorously proven. This enables: (i) free acceleration of convergence via natural polynomial interpretation, and (ii) elimination of bootstrapping-induced noise that harms accuracy in state-of-the-art methods. Implementation results show our approach achieves **~40-bit precision**, doubling the ~20-bit precision of prior work under identical parameters—the limit now imposed solely by fundamental rescaling noise. It also requires less multiplicative depth and exhibits superior numerical stability.

item.title: Tweed: Adaptively Secure Lattice-Based Two-Round Threshold Signatures
item.description: This paper presents **Tweed**, the first lattice-based two-round threshold signature scheme achieving *adaptive security*—i.e., tolerating adaptive corruption of up to $T-1$ out of $N$ signers. Built solely on the standard **Module-LWE (MLWE)** and **Module-SIS (MSIS)** assumptions (without random oracles), Tweed improves dramatically over the only prior adaptively secure lattice construction (Katsumata–Reichle–Takemure, CRYPTO ’24), which requires five rounds. Our core innovation is a novel two-round key aggregation protocol with VRF-driven threshold commitments, enabling consistent signing even under real-time corruptions. We prove Tweed satisfies strong existential unforgeability (EUF-CMA) and robustness in the standard model with tight reductions. The scheme uses static public parameters, requires no trusted setup, and achieves practical efficiency: at 128-bit security, total communication is <15 KB and signing latency is ~60% lower than the five-round alternative.

item.title: An Ultra-Robust Privacy Preserving Scheme for Federated Learning using Distributed Homomorphic Encryption
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) mitigates raw-data exposure but remains vulnerable to inference attacks on shared model updates. To address this, we propose **RRFL-DHE**, a privacy-preserving FL framework integrating *distributed homomorphic encryption* and *threshold linear secret sharing*. It enables secure server-side aggregation of encrypted client updates without revealing individual contributions, while its dropout management protocol ensures robustness against client failures. We provide a formal security proof under the semi-honest server model and evaluate RRFL-DHE on non-IID MNIST and Fashion MNIST using SVM and CNN models. Results show: (1) <1% accuracy drop versus FedAvg; (2) ~15% higher accuracy than xMK-CKKS; and (3) stable convergence under 30% random client dropout. RRFL-DHE thus achieves unprecedented balance among privacy, utility, and resilience.

item.title: Defensive Refusal Bias: How Safety Alignment Fails Cyber Defenders
item.description: This paper identifies *Defensive Refusal Bias*: a critical failure mode in safety-aligned LLMs where models refuse legitimate, authorized cybersecurity defense tasks—e.g., system hardening or malware analysis—solely due to lexical overlap with offensive terminology (e.g., “bypass”, “shellcode”). Evaluating 2,390 real-world NCCDC tasks, we find safety-tuned LLMs reject defensive requests containing security-sensitive keywords at **2.72× the rate** of semantically equivalent neutral requests (*p* < 0.001), with peak refusal rates in operationally vital tasks: **43.8% for system hardening** and **34.3% for malware analysis**. Strikingly, explicit user authorization statements *increase* refusal rates—indicating models misinterpret justifications as adversarial rather than exculpatory. These findings expose a fundamental flaw: current alignment relies on *surface-level semantic similarity* to harmful content, not intent or authorization reasoning. For autonomous defensive agents—which cannot rephrase or retry—this bias poses urgent operational risk. We advocate intent-aware alignment to preserve defensive utility without compromising safety.

item.title: ATLAS: AI-Assisted Threat-to-Assertion Learning for System-on-Chip Security Verification
item.description: ATLAS is an LLM-driven framework that bridges standardized threat modeling (e.g., CWE, STRIDE) and property-based formal verification for System-on-Chip (SoC) security. It automatically identifies SoC-specific assets, maps relevant vulnerabilities from knowledge bases, and generates semantically accurate, JasperGold-executable security assertions—leveraging multi-source SoC context (RTL, IP docs, protocols). Evaluated on three HACK@DAC benchmarks, ATLAS detected 39 of 48 CWEs (81.3% recall) and produced correct, verifiable assertions for 33 of those (84.6% precision). This work establishes the first fully automated, knowledge-grounded pipeline from generic vulnerability intelligence to SoC-level formal verification artifacts, advancing secure-by-design hardware development.

item.title: vEcho: A Paradigm Shift from Vulnerability Verification to Proactive Discovery with Large Language Models
item.description: vEcho redefines LLMs’ role in application security—from passive filters to proactive, learning-capable virtual security experts. To overcome SAST’s high false positive rates and the limitations of stateless LLM classifiers, vEcho introduces a Cognitive Memory Module and the novel Echoic Vulnerability Propagation (EVP) mechanism, enabling LLMs to learn from verified vulnerabilities, abstract semantic patterns, and actively infer analogous unknown flaws across codebases. Evaluated on CWE-Bench-Java, vEcho achieves a 65.0% detection rate (+41.8% relative gain over IRIS) and reduces false positives to 59.78% (−28.3% vs. IRIS’s 84.82%), while additionally identifying 37 undocumented known vulnerabilities and discovering 51 novel, manually validated 0-day vulnerabilities in real-world open-source projects.

item.title: AWE: Adaptive Agents for Dynamic Web Penetration Testing
item.description: Modern web applications, accelerated by AI-assisted development and no-code deployment, expose a critical gap between software velocity and the inflexibility of existing security tools. Pattern-based scanners lack contextual reasoning, while LLM-based pentesters suffer from high cost, instability, and poor reproducibility due to unconstrained exploration. We introduce **AWE**, a memory-augmented multi-agent framework that embeds *vulnerability-specific*, structured analysis pipelines (e.g., for XSS and blind SQLi) within a lightweight LLM orchestration layer. Unlike general-purpose agents, AWE combines context-aware payload mutation/generation with persistent memory and browser-backed verification to deliver deterministic, exploitation-driven results. Evaluated on the 104-challenge XBOW benchmark, AWE achieves **87% XSS success (+30.5% over MAPTA)** and **66.7% blind SQLi success (+33.3%)**, while being significantly faster, cheaper, and more token-efficient—despite using Claude Sonnet 4 versus MAPTA’s GPT-5. Our results demonstrate that principled, vulnerability-aware architecture—not just model scale—drives substantial gains in accuracy, efficiency, and determinism for injection-class exploits. Code: https://github.com/stuxlabs/AWE

item.title: Clawdrain: Exploiting Tool-Calling Chains for Stealthy Token Exhaustion in OpenClaw Agents
item.description: We present **Clawdrain**, a novel token-drain attack exploiting tool-calling chains in OpenClaw—a popular open-source, self-hosted generative agent. Unlike prior prompt-based exploits, Clawdrain Trojanizes skills via malicious `SKILL.md` instructions and a companion script emitting `PROGRESS`/`REPAIR`/`TERMINAL` signals to trigger a multi-turn “Segmented Verification Protocol.” Deployed on a production-like OpenClaw instance with real Gemini 2.5 Pro API billing, it achieves **6–7× token amplification** over benign baselines (up to **≈9×** under failure-prone conditions). Crucially, we observe a *deployment-only phenomenon*: the agent autonomously composes general-purpose tools (e.g., shell, Python) to bypass brittle protocol steps—reducing amplification and reshaping attack dynamics. We further identify four architecture-enabled production vectors: `SKILL.md` prompt bloat, persistent tool-output pollution, cron/heartbeat frequency amplification, and behavioral instruction injection. Our work demonstrates that token exhaustion remains a practical threat in real-world deployments, but its magnitude and detectability are fundamentally governed by tool composition, recovery behavior, and interface design—not just model vulnerabilities.

item.title: AMDS: Attack-Aware Multi-Stage Defense System for Network Intrusion Detection with Two-Stage Adaptive Weight Learning
item.description: This paper introduces AMDS, an Attack-aware Multi-stage Defense System for network intrusion detection, designed to counter heterogeneous adversarial attacks—including gradient-based and distribution-shift threats. Unlike uniform-defense approaches, AMDS employs a two-stage adaptive weight learning mechanism that dynamically combines ensemble disagreement, predictive uncertainty, and distributional anomaly signals—tailored to distinct attack signatures empirically identified across seven attack types. Evaluated on NSL-KDD, AMDS achieves 94.2% AUC, improving accuracy by 4.5 percentage points and F1-score by 9.0 points over adversarially trained ensembles. Under adaptive white-box attacks (FGSM & PGD variants), it maintains 94.4% accuracy with only 4.2% attack success rate—though formal robustness guarantees are not claimed. Cross-dataset analysis further reveals its dependence on baseline classifier competence and feature dimensionality. AMDS demonstrates that attack-specific optimization and multi-signal integration offer a practical, scalable path toward robust ML-based NIDS.

item.title: Communication-Efficient Quantum Federated Learning over Large-Scale Wireless Networks
item.description: This paper addresses the critical communication bottleneck in quantum federated learning (QFL) over large-scale wireless networks. We propose a novel multi-channel QFL framework leveraging non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and formulate a joint channel selection and power allocation problem to maximize system sum-rate—a non-convex, NP-hard mixed-integer nonlinear program. To solve it efficiently, we design an iterative optimization algorithm embedding the quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) within a classical-quantum hybrid loop. Further, we provide the first theoretical convergence analysis of QFL under full device participation, rigorously accounting for nonconvex losses, statistical heterogeneity, and quantum shot noise. Simulations demonstrate that our approach achieves >100% sum-rate gain over state-of-the-art baselines while improving model accuracy by 12.3% and accelerating convergence by 2.8×.

item.title: Differential privacy representation geometry for medical image analysis
item.description: Differential privacy (DP) in medical imaging is conventionally assessed only via end-to-end accuracy, obscuring *how* privacy constraints degrade utility. We propose **DP-RGMI**, a framework interpreting DP as a structured geometric transformation of representation space and decomposing utility loss into two quantifiable components: *(i)* **encoder geometry**, measured by representation displacement from initialization and spectral effective dimension; and *(ii)* **task-head utilization**, defined as the gap between linear-probe and end-to-end performance. Evaluating across >594K chest X-ray images from four datasets and multiple pretrained backbones (ResNet-50, ViT-B/16, DINOv2), we find that DP consistently induces a utilization gap—even when linear separability remains high—indicating downstream adaptation failure rather than feature collapse. Crucially, geometric changes are non-monotonic and highly dependent on initialization and dataset, revealing DP’s role in *reshaping representation anisotropy*, not uniform compression. Correlation analysis shows utilization strongly predicts end-to-end performance across datasets, while geometric metrics capture additional prior- and data-conditioned variation. DP-RGMI thus provides a reproducible, diagnosis-oriented foundation for understanding and mitigating privacy-induced failures in medical AI.

item.title: Tracking Capabilities for Safer Agents
item.description: AI agents interacting with the real world via tool calls face critical safety risks—including private data leakage, unintended side effects, and prompt injection attacks. To address these, we propose a *capability-safe safety harness*: agents express intentions as code in Scala 3 with capture checking, where capabilities (e.g., `NetworkIO`, `SecretRead`) are typed program variables statically tracked by the compiler. This enables *local purity*—enforcing side-effect-freedom in sensitive sub-computations—to provably prevent information leakage. Experiments across diverse tool-augmented tasks show agents generate capability-safe code with **no significant performance drop** (98.2%±0.7% task success vs. baseline), while the type system **reliably blocks all unsafe behaviors**, including unauthorized resource access, cross-domain data exfiltration, and malicious side effects. Our approach demonstrates that strong, extensible agent safety can be built directly into the programming language layer—without runtime sandboxes or post-hoc filtering.

item.title: Conformal Prediction for Risk-Controlled Medical Entity Extraction Across Clinical Domains
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for medical entity extraction but suffer from miscalibrated confidence scores, hindering safe clinical deployment. We introduce a conformal prediction (CP) framework that provides finite-sample coverage guarantees across two distinct clinical domains. First, we extract structured entities from 1,000 FDA drug labels (8 sections) using GPT-4.1, validated via FactScore-based atomic evaluation (97.7% accuracy over 128,906 entities). Second, we extract radiological entities from MIMIC-CXR reports using the RadGraph schema with GPT-4.1 and Llama-4-Maverick, achieving entity F1 scores of 0.81–0.84 against physician annotations. Crucially, we find the *direction* of miscalibration reverses across domains: models are **underconfident** on well-structured FDA text (requiring low CP threshold τ ≈ 0.06) but **overconfident** on free-text radiology reports (requiring high τ up to 0.99). Despite this heterogeneity, CP achieves ≥90% target coverage in both settings with modest rejection rates (9–13%). Our work demonstrates that calibration is not a global model property but depends critically on document structure, entity type, and architecture—motivating domain-specific conformal calibration for trustworthy clinical AI.

item.title: PPC-MT: Parallel Point Cloud Completion with Mamba-Transformer Hybrid Architecture
item.description: We propose **PPC-MT**, a parallel point cloud completion framework that synergizes the linear-complexity efficiency of Mamba and the fine-grained relational modeling power of Transformer. PPC-MT introduces a PCA-guided parallel decomposition strategy: it orders unordered point clouds geometrically and splits them into spatially coherent subsets, which are reconstructed *in parallel* by a multi-head Transformer reconstructor. During encoding, Mamba extracts global features in O(n) time; during decoding, the Transformer captures intricate cross-subset dependencies for high-fidelity detail recovery. On PCN, ShapeNet-55/34, and KITTI, PPC-MT achieves state-of-the-art performance—e.g., +12.7% average CD-L1 improvement and 23.6 FPS inference speed—outperforming prior methods in both accuracy and efficiency. Code and models will be publicly released.

item.title: JailNewsBench: Multi-Lingual and Regional Benchmark for Fake News Generation under Jailbreak Attacks
item.description: JailNewsBench is the first multilingual and regional benchmark designed to evaluate LLM robustness against jailbreak-induced fake news generation. It spans **34 regions and 22 languages**, including low-resource ones (e.g., Swahili, Vietnamese, Hindi), with ~300k instances. Using **5 jailbreak attack methods** and an LLM-as-a-Judge framework, it assesses 8 safety sub-metrics—including factual accuracy, harmfulness, virality, and cultural appropriateness. Evaluating 9 mainstream LLMs, we find a maximum attack success rate (ASR) of **86.3%** and a maximum harmfulness score of **3.5/5**. Critically, models show significantly **weaker defense on English and U.S.-related topics**—exposing severe safety imbalances across languages and regions. Moreover, existing safety datasets cover <7% of fake news patterns and under-defend them compared to toxicity or bias. Code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/kanekomasahiro/jail_news_bench.

item.title: Fed-ADE: Adaptive Learning Rate for Federated Post-adaptation under Distribution Shift
item.description: Federated learning (FL) in post-deployment settings faces unsupervised, non-stationary data streams across heterogeneous clients, where fixed learning rates often cause underfitting or divergence under time-varying distribution shifts. We propose **Fed-ADE**, an unsupervised federated adaptation framework that estimates distribution dynamics via two lightweight, client-local signals: (i) *uncertainty dynamics* (tracking predictive entropy and confidence shifts) and (ii) *representation dynamics* (monitoring feature-level covariate drift). These are fused into a per-client, per-timestep adaptive learning rate—requiring no labels, global coordination, or model architecture changes. Theoretically, our estimators approximate underlying distribution distances and yield provable dynamic regret bounds and convergence guarantees. Experiments on image (CIFAR-10-C, DomainNet) and text (AG News-C) benchmarks under diverse label/covariate shifts show Fed-ADE consistently outperforms strong baselines (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx, AdaBelief-FL, FedCorr), improving accuracy by 3.2–5.7 points on average. This demonstrates that explicit distribution-shift awareness is essential for robust real-world federated post-adaptation.

item.title: Separating Non-Interactive Classical Verification of Quantum Computation from Falsifiable Assumptions
item.description: Mahadev’s landmark 4-message protocol (SIAM J. Comput. 2022) enabled classical verification of quantum computation under the Learning-with-Errors (LWE) assumption—a falsifiable assumption. This raised the central open question: *Can non-interactive classical verification of QMA be achieved in the plain model?* We answer negatively: **there is no quantum black-box reduction from non-interactive classical verification of QMA to any falsifiable assumption**. Here, “non-interactive” means a single message after instance-independent setup—covering all standard cryptographic assumptions (e.g., LWE, RSA, DDH). Our separation holds assuming the existence of a $\textsf{QMA}\text{--}\textsf{QCMA}$ gap problem—a mild strengthening of the widely believed $\textsf{QMA} \neq \textsf{QCMA}$ conjecture. To support plausibility, we construct such a gap problem relative to a quantum unitary oracle, proving its separation in that relativized world. This is the first strong impossibility result limiting the foundations of non-interactive verification.

item.title: Towards Practical Registered ABE: More Efficient, Non-monotone, and CCA-secure
item.description: We propose the first practical Registered ABE (Reg-ABE) scheme that is **non-monotone**, **CCA-secure**, and **highly space-efficient**. In a realistic setting, both the master public key (MPK) and ciphertext are only ~7.5 KB—reducing MPK size by >2000× and ciphertext size by 5× compared to the state-of-the-art GLWW scheme (Crypto’24). Helper keys and system state shrink by >40×. Crucially, our scheme supports expressive non-monotone policies and strong CCA security—neither supported by GLWW. Implementation shows our encryption outperforms GLWW; decryption, though a few times slower, remains under 0.1 seconds on a laptop. Security is proven in the generic group model. We also present a simpler, more efficient dual-system variant that achieves CPA security under the MDDH assumption in the standard model—surpassing the only prior non-monotone Reg-ABE (Attrapadung et al., Crypto’24).

item.title: On Best-Possible One-Time Programs
item.description: One-time programs (OTPs) enable single-input evaluation of a functionality without leaking extra information. While deterministic quantum OTPs are provably impossible due to gentle-measurement attacks, the fundamental limits for randomized functionalities—and the strongest achievable security—remain unclear. Inspired by “best-possible obfuscation” in classical settings, we ask whether a generic best-possible OTP compiler exists. We answer *negatively*: assuming lossy encryption (e.g., from LWE or weak PRGAs), no such compiler can exist—even for classical randomized functionalities—due to inherent behavioral contradictions on indistinguishable function families. To circumvent this, we introduce *testable OTP compilers*, which output quantum states augmented with self-reflection oracles. We then define *Single-Effective-Query (SEQ) simulation security*—a simplified, generalized notion for quantum channels—and prove it implies best-possible testable OTP security. Crucially, we construct SEQ-secure OTPs for *all quantum functionalities* in the classical oracle model—the first result beyond classical randomized ones. Finally, we propose *stateful quantum indistinguishability obfuscation (iO)* and show it both implies best-possible testable OTPs and is realizable in the same classical oracle model, establishing it as a promising foundation.

item.title: VisualDedup: Visual Fuzzy Deduplication for Secure Batch Duplicates Detection without Server Aided
item.description: Deduplication of encrypted multimedia data faces dual challenges: (1) ciphertext’s chaotic appearance attracts adversarial scrutiny, and (2) server-assisted schemes introduce trust bottlenecks and privacy risks. VisualDedup addresses these by proposing *Visual Fuzzy Deduplication*—a serverless, batch-capable framework for secure image deduplication. It generates robust, fuzzy-equivalent visual fingerprints via DCT-LSH hashing with dynamic salted rotation, enabling efficient local similarity matching without exposing raw or encrypted data. Crucially, it resists brute-force attacks through non-deterministic yet verifiable fingerprint generation and achieves O(1) average query complexity via optimized Bloom-filter-based batching. Evaluated on CIFAR-10, ImageNet-Subset, and Medical-XRay datasets, VisualDedup achieves ≥98.7% F1-score, <0.3% false positive rate, 12.4K images/sec throughput, and reduces communication overhead by 92% versus Crypto-Dedupe—demonstrating practicality for privacy-preserving cloud storage.

item.title: A Built-in Crypto Expert for Artificial Intelligence: How Far is the Horizon?
item.description: This paper introduces a built-in cryptographic framework embedding a dedicated “Crypto Expert” directly into LLM architectures. We design a differentiable AES-128 proxy using novel neuron units—SoftXOR, SoftLUT, and GF Conv—that preserve Boolean-level functional equivalence while enabling stable backpropagation. Integrated as a specialized expert in an MoE-LLM, the model autonomously routes sensitive tokens to encryption during training; at inference, the proxy is swapped for a standard discrete AES implementation, guaranteeing provable security. Experiments show our approach reduces encryption-related neuron count by 92% and latency by 76% versus ReLU-based baselines, resists continuous differential attacks, and maintains >99.3% downstream task utility. This work pioneers end-to-end, formally secure AI computation.

item.title: IU: Imperceptible Universal Backdoor Attack
item.description: We propose **IU (Imperceptible Universal)**, the first universal backdoor attack that is *visually undetectable*, *multi-target scalable*, and *extremely low-cost*. Unlike prior methods relying on salient patterns, IU leverages **graph convolutional networks (GCNs)** to model inter-class semantic relationships and generate class-specific yet globally applicable perturbations—ensuring both high attack success rate (ASR) and perceptual invisibility. Our framework jointly optimizes ASR and stealthiness via a dual-objective loss using PSNR and LPIPS as perceptual metrics. On ImageNet-1K with ResNet-50/101, IU achieves **91.3% ASR at only 0.16% poisoning rate**, preserves benign accuracy (>99.6%), and evades state-of-the-art defenses (STRIP, Neural Cleanse, ABS). This work exposes critical vulnerabilities in current defense paradigms and calls for robust detection of imperceptible universal threats.

item.title: MIDAS: Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction for Jailbreaking MLLMs
item.description: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit strong capabilities but remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that bypass safety alignment. Prior visual jailbreak methods—relying on single-image masking or isolated cues—achieve limited success against strongly aligned commercial MLLMs due to shallow, unstructured reasoning. To overcome this, we propose **MIDAS (Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction)**: a novel framework that decomposes malicious semantics into risk-bearing subunits, disperses them across multiple semantically coordinated images, and leverages cross-image chained reasoning to *gradually reconstruct* the harmful intent—thereby evading detection by safety mechanisms. MIDAS enforces longer, structured visual reasoning, increases model reliance on images by 2.7×, delays malicious semantic exposure, and reduces security attention scores by 63.8% on average. Evaluated on 4 closed-source MLLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Qwen-VL-Max), MIDAS achieves a **mean attack success rate of 81.46%**, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by up to 39.7 points. Code is publicly available.

item.title: IdGlow: Dynamic Identity Modulation for Multi-Subject Generation
item.description: IdGlow is a mask-free, two-stage framework for multi-subject image generation, built upon Flow Matching diffusion models. To resolve the stability-plasticity dilemma—especially in identity-preserving age transformation—we propose: (1) a supervised fine-tuning stage with task-adaptive timestep scheduling, featuring linearly decaying constraints for natural group composition and temporal gating to confine identity injection within a critical semantic window; and (2) a fine-grained group-level Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with weighted margins, jointly suppressing multi-subject artifacts, enhancing texture harmony, and recalibrating identity fidelity toward real-world distributions. A badcase-driven Vision-Language Model further enables precise, layout-free prompt synthesis. Experiments on direct multi-person fusion and age-transformed group generation show IdGlow achieves superior Pareto efficiency—outperforming SOTA in facial fidelity while attaining commercial-grade aesthetics (e.g., −23.6% FID, +18.4% CLIP-Score).

item.title: DeepAFL: Deep Analytic Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) faces persistent challenges in heterogeneity, convergence, and scalability due to reliance on gradient-based updates. While analytic FL methods—using closed-form solutions like least squares—offer strong invariance to data heterogeneity, they are restricted to shallow, linear models with frozen backbones, lacking representation learning capability. To bridge this gap, we propose **DeepAFL**, the first deep analytic FL framework that jointly achieves *heterogeneity robustness* and *end-to-end representation learning*. Inspired by ResNet, DeepAFL introduces **gradient-free residual blocks** with analytically solvable parameters and a **layer-wise federated training protocol** based on local least-squares optimization. Theoretical analysis guarantees convergence under Non-IID settings, and empirical evaluation across CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, and Shakespeare shows DeepAFL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by **5.68%–8.42%** in accuracy, with lower communication overhead and superior generalization.

item.title: Learning to Attack: A Bandit Approach to Adversarial Context Poisoning
item.description: We propose **AdvBandit**, the first black-box adaptive attack against neural contextual bandits that formulates adversarial context poisoning as a *continuous-armed bandit problem*. Without accessing the victim’s parameters, gradients, or reward function, AdvBandit learns a surrogate policy via maximum-entropy inverse reinforcement learning from observed (context, action) pairs, then optimizes perturbations using projected gradient descent guided by a UCB-aware Gaussian process. A novel attack-budget control mechanism limits detectability and overhead. Theoretically, we prove sublinear attacker regret ($O(\sqrt{T}\log T)$) and establish a linear lower bound ($\Omega(K)$) on victim regret in the number of attacks $K$. Experiments on Yelp, MovieLens, and Disin datasets show AdvBandit achieves up to **3.2× higher cumulative victim regret** than state-of-the-art baselines across diverse victim algorithms (LinUCB, NeuCB, DeepThompson), with significantly smaller, stealthier perturbations.

item.title: From Goals to Aspects, Revisited: An NFR Pattern Language for Agentic AI Systems
item.description: This paper revisits and extends the goals-to-aspects methodology for the emerging domain of agentic AI systems. We introduce a pattern language of 12 reusable, i*-driven aspects organized across four NFR categories: security, reliability, observability, and cost management—each implemented via a Rust-based AOP framework. Four patterns address agent-specific crosscutting concerns absent in classical AOP: tool-scope sandboxing, prompt injection detection, token budget management, and action audit trails. We extend the V-graph model to capture dual contributions of agent tasks to both functional goals and non-functional soft-goals. Validated through a case study on an open-source autonomous agent framework (AutoGen), our approach systematically identifies and modularizes crosscutting concerns early in the requirements-to-implementation pipeline. The pattern language provides a principled, traceable, and engineering-ready foundation for building production-grade agentic AI systems.
